topics.title: System Design Fundamentals — Scalability, Caching & Load Balancing
topics.description: System design is about making architectural decisions that allow a system to handle growth (scalability), stay available (reliability), and respond fast (performance). The core toolkit: horizontal scaling (add more servers), load balancing (distribute traffic), caching (serve from memory), CDN (edge content), database indexing, and async processing (message queues). Every design decision is a trade-off — know the axes: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput, cost vs performance.
topics.story: Your app has 10 users — one server, one database, done. You hit TechCrunch — 100,000 users in an hour. The server melts. System design is the art of building the scaffolding BEFORE the fire alarm goes off. It's not about clever algorithms — it's about: where is the bottleneck? How do I add more of the slow thing? How do I stop asking the slow thing at all? The answers are always the same patterns: cache, queue, shard, replicate, CDN. The skill is knowing when and how to apply each.
topics.analogy: Vertical scaling = hiring a superhuman employee (bigger machine). Horizontal scaling = hiring more average employees (more servers). Load balancer = the receptionist who routes customers to available staff. Cache = a whiteboard where you write answers to frequent questions so staff don't have to look them up. CDN = branch offices in every city so customers don't all queue at HQ. Message queue = in-tray on the receptionist's desk — staff process at their own pace. Database replica = photocopies of the filing cabinet for read-heavy staff.
topics.memoryAnchor: Vertical scaling: bigger machine. Horizontal: more machines + load balancer. LB algorithms: round-robin, least-connections, IP hash (sticky sessions). Cache levels: L1 CPU → L2 → L3 → RAM → Redis → CDN. Cache patterns: cache-aside (app manages), write-through (write to cache+DB), write-back (write cache first, async to DB). Cache eviction: LRU, LFU, TTL. CDN: static assets, geographic distribution, edge cache. Message queue: decouples producer/consumer. Throughput: requests/sec. Latency: ms per request. p99: 99th percentile latency.
topics.firstPrinciples: Every bottleneck is either CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network I/O. Identify the bottleneck first. Caching reduces DB load by serving from memory (RAM ~100ns vs SSD ~100µs vs HDD ~10ms). Horizontal scaling works for stateless services; stateful services need sticky sessions or externalized state. Asynchronous processing increases throughput by decoupling slow operations from the request path.
topics.bruteForce: Single server: handles everything (web + app + DB). Works at small scale. Fails when: DB becomes bottleneck (read-heavy → add read replicas), app server CPU saturated (horizontal scale + LB), data grows too large for one DB (sharding needed). Single point of failure — no fault tolerance.
topics.optimizedApproach: Layered scaling: LB → app servers (horizontal, stateless) → Redis cache → primary DB + read replicas → CDN for static assets → message queue for async jobs. Each layer scales independently. Identify the slowest component and optimize that first — premature optimization of the wrong layer wastes time.
topics.whenToUse: Cache: read-heavy data that changes rarely (user profiles, product catalog, session data). Message queue: slow operations that don't need synchronous response (email, notifications, video encoding). CDN: static assets, large media, geographically distributed users. Read replicas: read/write ratio > 3:1. Sharding: single DB > 500GB or write throughput maxed. Microservices: teams > 10 engineers, independent deployment requirements.

examples.title: Cache-Aside Pattern with TTL
examples.explanation: Cache-aside (lazy loading): app first checks cache, on miss goes to DB and populates cache. App controls the cache. Write: update DB first, then invalidate/update cache. TTL prevents stale data accumulation. The DB is only hit on cache misses — with 80% cache hit rate, DB load drops by 80%. Redis implements this pattern with O(1) get/set and distributed expiry.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: topic content cached in Redis (rarely changes). User profile cached after login. Quiz questions cached. Cache key: 'topic:{id}', 'user:{id}', 'quiz:active'. Cache invalidation: update topic in admin → delete 'topic:{id}' from Redis. Spring Cache abstraction: @Cacheable('topics') on service method — no cache code in business logic.

examples.title: Load Balancer Algorithms — Round Robin & Least Connections
examples.explanation: Round Robin distributes evenly — good when requests have similar cost. Least Connections adapts to actual load — better when requests vary in duration (some long, some short). IP Hash (consistent hashing) routes same client to same server — needed for sticky sessions. Weighted Round Robin assigns more requests to powerful servers. AWS ALB uses Round Robin by default, Least Outstanding Requests for variable workloads.
examples.realWorldUse: AWS ALB: distributes traffic across ECS tasks/EC2 instances. Health checks remove unhealthy targets. Sticky sessions for session-based apps (not needed with JWT). Nginx: upstream block with round-robin. Rate limiting per IP: nginx limit_req_zone. Blue-green deployment: switch LB to new target group with zero downtime.

problems.title: LRU Cache Implementation
problems.hint1: LinkedHashMap(capacity, 0.75, true) with accessOrder=true auto-maintains LRU order.
problems.hint2: Override removeEldestEntry to return true when size > capacity.
problems.hint3: O(1) with doubly-linked list + HashMap: map stores key→node, list maintains LRU order.
problems.sampleInput: LRUCache(2): put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2)

problems.title: Rate Limiter — Fixed Window Counter
problems.hint1: Fixed window: reset counter every windowMs milliseconds.
problems.hint3: If windowStart + windowMs < now → new window, reset count. Else increment.
problems.sampleInput: RateLimiter(10, 60000ms): 11 requests from same client in 1 minute

problems.title: Consistent Hashing — Distribute Keys Across Servers
problems.hint1: Hash each server to multiple positions on a ring (virtual nodes) using TreeMap<Integer,String>.
problems.hint2: getServer(key): hash the key, find the next server clockwise with treeMap.ceilingKey(hash), wrap around with firstKey() if needed.
problems.hint3: Virtual nodes (e.g., server + '#1', '#2'...) ensure even distribution.
problems.testCases: Routes to one of S1/S2/S3. After removing S2, same key routes to S1 or S3.
problems.sampleInput: 3 servers, add server4; key 'user:123' routes to server2 before, server2 after (minimal disruption)
problems.sampleOutput: ~25% of keys remapped (1/N where N=4); rest stay on same server
